summary: Visibility for origin traffic
description:
Aha Ticket - https://akamai.aha.io/features/COREREL-23

The Akamai platform has no breakdown for the failed requests (requests with status code: 5xx’s). It's required to be able to separate between failures that are happened on the platform (because of the platform issues) and failures which have arrived from the origin itself. 5xx visibility is also a WS6 ask [line 13 https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1kGZf4roU_6V9jpH_0D6ICG021FkebAof__UwcjjzFCI/edit#gid=2089291414|https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1kGZf4roU_6V9jpH_0D6ICG021FkebAof__UwcjjzFCI/edit#gid=2089291414]

[ COREREL-22|https://akamai.aha.io/features/COREREL-22?reference-class=Feature] feature covers offering that visibility on the forward side along with solving for multi-origin/multi-CDN failover issues.

 

However last-mile visibility of Akamai caused errors, at the Edge, is also central to differentiating customer issues from Akamai ones. It's required to add these missing visibilities in order to allow us as platform to minimize the amount of failures which happens on the platform as a result of platform and improve the end user performance when fail back policies are entering to action.
creator: Devashish Thakur [X]

summary: Cryptoserver Performance Issues
description:
~70% of FF hostnames and ~45% of hostnames on ESSL don't use Pconn. This means that every request does a DNS lookup and SSL handshake for such hostnames.

While investigating FF SSL Overhead , we have found that we dont map to the cryptoserver closest to the edge server. This leads to 2x or 3x increase in cryptoserver latency.

We also dont seem to have cryptoserver capacity in all the major locations where we have MLEs. The goal of this ticket is two fold -
 # Improve mapping of cryptoserver to improve SSL handshake latency.

 # Wider deployment of cryptoserver in all location where we have large MLEs.

More details of performance impact can be found here - [https://collaborate.akamai.com/confluence/display/MSPFNDTN/SSL+Performance+Analysis+-+Freeflow]

Aha Ticket - [https://akamai.aha.io/features/COREPERF-76]

Aha idea - [COREPERF-I-203|https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/COREPERF-I-203]
resolution_description: This should be part of the Cryptoserver performance outcome (Li-Wei’s group).
creator: Devashish Thakur [X]

summary: Impact of KRP on customer Handshake Performance
description:
Aha Ticket - [https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/COREPERF-I-228]

 

A lot of customers on ESSL dont use persistent connections. If there are lot of keysigning requests on a slot , on ESSL KRP (Keysign Robustness Protection) will kick in and SSL handshake performance will degrade. 

We need to find out how often KRP kicks in , which customers are impacted and what can we do to reduce the impact.

summary: Improve In-Metro Mapping
description:
Aha Ticket - [https://akamai.aha.io/features/COREPERF-55]  is just a POC - Shipped

https://akamai.aha.io/features/COREPERF-55 is just a POC. ERECSO-16 is a platform problem. We haven't solved in-metro mapping on the platform.

Aha Ticket - [https://akamai.aha.io/features/COREPERF-119 |https://akamai.aha.io/features/COREPERF-119]

This is part of 18 month roadmap. It is prioritized. Mihai is finalizing the scope. 

*Business Hypothesis:*

We currently map ~15-40% of traffic out metro. The range depends on ASN and Maprule combination. It is hard to get a metro view however if we use state boundaries as a coarse proxy this view can provide us some signal:

[https://www.nocc.akamai.com/miniurl/?id=1f682]

If we can make improvements toward lowering this out of state/ out of metro mapping should result in customers connecting to closer edges. This will drive down edge RTT and improve throughput and transfer times.

 

{*}Acceptance Criteria{*}:
 * We will establish an auditable set of rules per continent for "what is local" eg. In the US all local name servers must be w/in 300 miles of the region.

 * We have a way to report on which names ervers are local to which regions so that we can audit completeness and audit in the future

 

*Additional info:*

When we create Edge maps , we have 2 kinds of bonus local and non-local. As an example FL nameserver should be local to MLEs in MIA and non-local for MLEs in ATL.

Local bonus to maps would prefer local regions. This would help if for some reason a bad pingpoint is chosen and local/non-local region have same ping score. Local bonus also improves stability in mapping.

The locality of regions is broken today , specially in US. A COX nameserver in Arizona is marked local to all regions in US (Chicago , Florida) etc. Similarly a TWC namserver in FL is marked local to all regions in US.

 

This may include enhancements to the model by which the existing "locality" relation is established or come up with a new "proximity" relation.

 

Below are some examples where nameservers are marked local to all regions in US.

 

TWC in FL -[ [https://map.akamai.com/Products/Mapper/Tools/IpInfo/index.php?EdgeScapePro=on&RapScoresPre=on&IpAddr=75.114.90.72]|https://map.akamai.com/Products/Mapper/Tools/IpInfo/index.php?EdgeScapePro=on&RapScoresPre=on&IpAddr=75.114.90.72]

Cox in AZ - [https://map.akamai.com/Products/Mapper/Tools/IpInfo/index.php?EdgeScapePro=on&RapScoresPre=on&IpAddr=184.178.231.18]

Comcast in MA - [https://map.akamai.com/Products/Mapper/Tools/IpInfo/index.php?EdgeScapePro=on&RapScoresPre=on&IpAddr=69.252.230.199]

We would like the broken locality of nameservers to be fixed , where regions local to the nameserver are the only one's marked local. This would improve RTT significantly and it will also complement edge scoring , where edgescoring can pick best performing local regions rather than trying to find best performing regions from all across the country.

 

In-state mapping in US for major maps: [https://www.nocc.akamai.com/miniurl/?id=20ebe]

summary: Metrocache as a replacement for TD0 maps
description:
Aha - [https://akamai.aha.io/features/COREREL-15]

 

Our concept of a modernized distributed, fast caching and offloading model that reduces manual operational interventions and improves long-tail offload % for customers - moves out of experimental rollout phase (last one [ DEL-191|https://akamai.aha.io/features/DEL-191?reference-class=Feature] ) .. to its first phase of global adoption in limited availability.

 

*In scope:*
 # Functionality carry-over from traditional Tier-Distribution for maps in scope

 # Complete install base adoption of medium and small traffic [CacheH|https://collaborate.akamai.com/confluence/display/STEFF/Maps+critical+to+product+health#Mapscriticaltoproducthealth-CacheH] maps to reduce decay issues and reactive operational overhead, in-turn improving reliability significantly

 # Deprecation and folding in of PCH, will pick from scope and value of [ DEL-482|https://akamai.aha.io/features/DEL-482?reference-class=Feature]

 # Turn key enablement for all new customers

 # Performance and health monitoring tools

 # Functionality that sets the stage for consistent customer origin offload feature development

 # Clear guidelines for exceptions to MCH cachefill (e.g. where we will use cacheH maps)

 

*Out of scope for this phase*
 # Top 9 customer-conglomerates, these are assumed to be already performance and offload optimized by GSS and PRE involvement and customizations. Other than any exceptions called out by PRE team where significant performance and reliability benefit is expected from MCH beyond their existing setups.

 # Customers with no existing TD offload-tiering (SS, CW etc don't count)

 # Additional functionality than what’s available on tier distribution today

 # Compensation for origin performance issues

 # Mandated performance gains everywhere

 # Origin offload goal being accomplished. Though it is expected to improve in some localities while not degrade anywhere.

 # Operationalizing to Edge/TD TAT. 

 # Drastic Offload degradations will be alerted on, but time series offload needs tooling.

 

*Long-term goals(next phases):*
 # Complete install base adoption including Top 9 and customers on legacy products

 # Customers with no existing TD offload-tiering will enjoy a default TD through MCH

 # Deprecation of traditional and siloed tiers eg: TD0, PCH etc. from operations

 # Attainment of Customer offload goals in combination with other solutions

 # All tier country support into capacity plans like Edge and TD today, including deployment policies and cost modeling for future metros (cost-performance trade-offs)

 # Geo and product specific KPIs established and met(will impact MCH and non-MCH TD0).

 # Operational Mgmt of MCH established (SRMM config, metrocache, data management, etc.)

 

*Business Hypothesis*

1. Improved and more consistent tier offload

2. Reduced complexity and manual intervention for managing and configuring maps

3. Improve platform load-sharing via a more distributed caching model at the edge - reduced load being focused a small number of large "catch-all" regions

4. Improvements in long-tail catchebility with no negative impact on Product KPIs

5. Ability to better plan strategic innovations out from this. 

6. Allow us to improve the quality of our cache H deployment and planning process

7. Improved reliability and cost boundaries

8 . Reduction of capacity over-provisioning risk

summary: Investigate and Mitigate Overloading of regions by Mapper
description:
Aha - [https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/COREREL-I-38]

The ERE team get a number of escalation on mapper overloading regions and making regions serve traffic beyond the soft cap of the region. This causes ghost rolls and inmany cases results in 5xx being delivered to the regions.

Some of the issues are highlighted -
 # Mapper overloading region or link during large events
 # Mapper overloading region due to spiky traffic.
 # Region overloaded because mapper wasnt fast enough to move traffic around non-loadbalanced load.
 # High flit manifest traffic overloading a region.
 # Multiple high flit traffic on the same region (mm2 , chc1) causing region overload.
 # Spiky sureroute traffic overloading a region and impacting other customers sharing the same region due.

 

ERE today mitigates it by lowering the FOM mark of the region , but that is not a scalable solution.

We need to find a solution for all of the above problems and there wont be one solution that fixes all the problems , so i see this aha more as an epic rather than an individual ticket.
creator: Devashish Thakur [X]

summary: Improve Platform instability caused by Ghost Rolls and Self Suspensions
description:
Aha - https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/COREREL-I-30


We observe a lot of customer escalations which gets tied back to ghost rolls (memtotals, asserts) and self suspensions on the platform. 

We see the rolls/suspensions impacting performance and reliability of end user traffic with increase in platform metrics such as [SSL handshake failures|https://collaborate.akamai.com/confluence/display/MSPFNDTN/SSL+Error+Codes#SSLErrorCodes-Conclusion], High connection establishment time (time_established, time_received_data) which happens before ghost accepts the connection. 

For products which rely of small set of regions to be healthy, the impacts of ghost instability due to rolls/suspensions is severe.

E.g., [CAC Issue to rolls caused by other traffic|https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/COREREL-I-29], [Impact of instability on API Core regions|https://collaborate.akamai.com/confluence/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=654439701], which is critical for API-X product[1][2][3].

[1] [https://collaborate.akamai.com/confluence/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=654439701]

[2] [https://collaborate.akamai.com/confluence/display/MSPFNDTN/Impact+on+API+core+regions#ImpactonAPIcoreregions-Ghostrollingandsuspensions]

[3] [https://collaborate.akamai.com/confluence/display/MSPFNDTN/TikTok+Video%3A+ID#TikTokVideo:ID-Ghostrolls]

summary: Serverside Visibility into SSL Slowness
description:
[https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/COREPERF-I-224]

*Issue:* We need to increase the visibility on the server side during SSL handshakes. There is a variable called 'mdpc' that is supposed to log one aspect of server-side slowness, i.e. metadata loading during SSL handshakes on SNI slots. But we have evidence to suggest that 'mdpc' logging has issues because we see *NO LOGGING* for our competitive tests in ESSL r-lines.

And this variable has been completely disabled on Freeflow, so even if the logging happened accurately we would not see it in Freeflow, where *ALL* handshakes are SNI.

 ** 

*What we have seen*

A definite correlation between ssl handshake time and metadata file size. When we reduced the metadata on ESSL from ~15K lines to ~7K lines the SSL handshake times *dropped by 20-25% at p50 and 20-40% at p95*.

On FF the metadata file size was reduced from ~15K lines to ~4K lines, and the SSL handshakes times dropped by *25-50% from p50 to p95*.

 

We see correlation of ssh handshake times with the 'a' component of the slhds field in r-77, (i.e. Client Hello to Server Hello Done). Values of slhds-a are in the multiple 10s of milliseconds, some even 100+ ms. But, no logging of 'mdpc' in r-lines.

 

*ESSL Products:* Ion and DSA.

*FF Products:* API, AMD

summary: FPJ Design Issues
description:
Aha! Idea - https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/COREREL-I-42

Ghost FPJ design has bunch of issues as listed below leading to either stale content , purge sequence errors or have incompatibility with other features.

 Below are the list of known issue raised for improvements of FPJ/Jetstream which can help address the issues and resolve different customer impacts caused.
 # https://track.akamai.com/jira/browse/GHOST-1012 related to slow purge history catch up.

 # [ PURGE-I-6|https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/PURGE-I-6?reference-class=Ideas::Idea] Improve Store entry sequence number restoration after ghost restart, which causes cache invalidation and increases origin hits.

 # https://track.akamai.com/jira/browse/GHOST-534 Avoid 5xx by Handling purge validation like SS parent in cacheh

 # https://track.akamai.com/jira/browse/WEBGESC-314 CacheTag and QR incompatibility.

 # Ghost is doing lazy evaluation of content tag purge, i.e. its not execute it immediately but keeps the purge instruction in the memory, and does the actual purge when the object is requested. Ghost also has a compaction mechanism for the tags, it trigger by time (every 8-10 hours), or when the tags memory size is above 16MB (Controlled by the config <config:fast-purge.content-tag.tags-mem-soft-limit default=16MB> ) Sometimes if the tags grow too quickly, Ghost does not succeed to do the compaction and after the compaction the tags db is still above the threshold. There is also hard limit, set to 32MB ( <config:fast-purge.content-tag.tags-mem-hard-limit default=32MB> 

So if any machine can roll before the 8h limit and in case purge happens before the purge history catches up , Ghost may serve stale metadata also .

summary: Midgress region overload due to SR/SS traffic
description:
Tracking region overload caused due to SR/SS traffic spikes.

 

Top Themes - 
1) When SR traffic co-exists with other edge/TD traffic, it can sometimes have unintended side effects. One of the major issue noticed is localized region overloads. As SR tries to optmize the origin path from edges for non-cacbeable content, certain last hop ecors/regions can take in exessive traffic. The reaction times to shift/effectively load balance SR traffic is not as aggressive as RA controlled traffic, resulting in extended periods of region instability. 

2) Overloads on SS - This is more likely to happen with custom SS maps, where the map has degraded over time or the SS maps haven't been updated to keep up with changing customer traffic profile, resulting in concentration of traffic on a subset of regions. This often also leads to imbalance in SS traffic across machines in a single region, which is tracked seprately in ERECSO-67. 

 

Challenges - 
1) No way to selectively suspend SR traffic from a region 
2) Event configs that have SR enabled, can result in more traffic concentrated on fewer last-hop regions. The impact is likely worse for events that sees large concurrency and/or fluctuating end user demand. Ex: Authentication, profile fetches during start/resumption or periodic refreshes.

 

For SR as a feature, last-hop load balancing could potentially be improved (specially when SS / ADC) by having some score quantization/ adjustments in place, so that difference of few ms could result in a larger set of candidate regions. Generally, both setups will have most of the regions with good connectivity to origin. Current setup tends to favor one region based on race stats, which gets overwhelmed with burst of requests under higher edge traffic conditions.

summary: Region Overload caused due to Large MPG's
description:
Tracking issues caused due to large traffic getting mapped to a region from large MPG's eventually causing customer performance issues.

While Fractional Assignment is globally enabled for all maprules, we continue to see issues where regions get overwhelmed due to a sudden influx of traffic from certain ASNs. Particularly, demand originating from cloud providers (AWS, GCP) seem to have fewer but larger MPGs. The issue is more significant for web/api maps that usually serve short lived, high f2b traffic. Things get worse if the requests don't arrive at a consistent rate, but fluctuate.

Load tests run from concentrated cloud agents often result in this problem. Majority of the demand gets mapped to a handful of regions, resulting in server overloads. While topology.xml exists for some of these larger providers today, this involves significant manual effort and is often updated reactively after an issue has already occurred. 

Large MPG problem isn't isolated to cloud providers. Certain end user ASNs in Korea, Vietnam have significant portion of their demand associated with a handful of large MPGs. 

Fractional Assignment doesn't seem to help in all scenarios - 1) When the demand is large enough that it cannot be fit into the current limit of 3 regions 2) Due to uFMP safeties, it might decide to map based on fallback.
creator: Mahesh Vijayan [X]

summary: Availability and performance issues due to WAF enabled traffic
description:
This tracks escalations as a result of certain customer's product features that adversely impacted the platform's performance and availability. This covers high flit resource consumption due to complex WAF rules, Manifest Manipulation etc

 

Top themes - 


1) Attack traffic on a WAF enabled property leading to significant flit resource utilization in a geo/map, starving genuine traffic of useable capacity


2) Enablement of certain WAF features, like ASE EVAL mode can drive up f2b significantly, leading to higher COGS and cannibalization of resources 


3) WAF enabled traffic that's generally spiky in nature or originates from concentrated sources, can trigger region-wide self-suspensions of machines 

 

Current Challenges - 


1) Attack response - Not all geos/network have sufficient headroom to absorb the attack. The quarantine maps are small and may not be an accurate way of handling customers' traffic with extended days of attack traffic given it also impacts genuine traffic. Need a standardized operational response to handle attack traffic. 


2) Lack of visibility into flit cost per major security features - When customers enable WAF and associated security features, there's no good understanding of the baseline flit cost, making it harder to estimate the potential impact of onboarding such traffic. The side-effects are only noticed after the traffic shows up with a higher flit cost, resulting in a forced roll back. It is also difficult to ascertain what security features could potentially be having the worst impact.
resolution_description: There have been no new escalations or repeat patterns observed in 2025, indicating that the issue is either resolved, stabilized, or no longer trending at a level that requires active tracking, or the actionables are being addressed as part of KTLO work.
creator: Sandeep Jain

summary: Improve Implementation of Forward Robustness protection (FRP)
description:
[https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/COREREL-I-48] 

Ghost is prevented from rolling when there are too many forward requests by FRP, or Forward Robustness Protection. Ghost will refuse any requests for a cpcode if it generates more than 12k (or another figure depending on the hardware defined in baseline).
This has an effect on all clients behind a cpcode even though it helps to increase ghost resilience.

Ghost has to be resilient, but we need to do it without impacting real customers.

summary: Single customer issues related to FSS
description:
https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/MD-I-20

Issues where customer noticed degradation either due to FSS (Forward Serial Selection) not being enabled or the default FSS not being enough.
 - For web products, there's no explicit FSS enabled on the forward requests. Cache able requests and those relying on query tags would benefit from having FSS enabled by effectively spreading both the footprint and hits across machines in a region. This improves offload and avoid overloading machines in the case of high f2h traffic. Potential risks: lower pconn

 - For media (AMD/DD), FSS is enabled by default. However for specific requests like manifests/SSAI, where the base URL (AK_PATH) doesn't change much, it might help to further hash using Ghost IP as another variable. The intent here is to address potential machine overload at the parent/CW tier. This could be enabled based on customer use case chosen in property manager.

 - Other FSS issues might step from an incorrect integration or lack of sufficient FSS range (ex: sharded PCH maps).
creator: Sandeep Jain

summary: Single customer issues related to Fail-action / Quick Retry
description:
https://akamai.aha.io/features/COREREL-19

Customer issues arising due to custom implementation of FA/QR in their ARL or the default settings being insufficient for their use case.

AMD/DD already offers default implementation of FA/QR, but many configs override this for a number of reasons -
 - Customers using segments sizes (3s, 5s etc) that aren't default in AMD. The cascade timeouts are defined assuming one of the predefined even values. They tend to override to values that are suitable for such sizes

 - QR is a standard 5Mbps across geos. With more customers adapting to UHD, this threshold is often insufficient to maintain the high KPIs customers expect, especially in T1 countries.

These custom implementations are sometimes not well understood by PS resulting in worse performance/resiliency than using the default. Excessive overrides result in complicated configs that are difficult to read and debug during issues.
creator: Sandeep Jain

summary: Single customer issues related to incorrect load reporting of multi CDN hostnames
description:
[https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/MD-I-19]

Hostnames that are load balanced among multiple CDNs or using GTM will need to include additional load reporting tags in the property to correctly report the incoming load on serial/map. This sometimes is missed either due to lack of awareness or an Akamai hostname switching to multiCDN in the future.

There are few variants of the tag. Either hard coding or not using the host header will also impact correct reporting of load at the Edge, eventually causing load balancing issues.

This is to explore the feasibility of a workaround or adding this tag automatically for customers that have multiCDN enabled hostnames.

tags suggested - <reporting:maprule> or  <reporting:maprule-host>

Expanding the Scope of ERECSO-69

Incorrect Load Reporting on Platform (ERECSO-22) 

Aha - [https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/COREPERF-I-230]

There are number of scenarios where enduser traffic are not reported on the platform. This happens on any of the below scenarios.
 # Customer sends traffic on multiCDN hostname and when ghost resolves the hostname it may not resolve to Akamai.

 # ZAM (Zone access mapping) is enabled on edge hostname

 # Sureroute load is reported on RA map.

We need to get the reporting issue fixed so that mapper can loadbalance and move traffic around. Currently all this traffic is unassigned load that mapper cannot move.

Also we need to design a fix that can be done outside of customer arl.data.
creator: Sandeep Jain

summary: Delivery impact by limited max-reconnect value
description:
The ERE team receives a number of escalation related to limited max-reconnect values in the AMD template. Current AMD delivery template limit max-reconnect to 1 (MDP-3465):
{code:xml}
$ head -121 delivery_amd_best_practices_v4.xml | tail
    <cache:enforce-single-refresh>on</cache:enforce-single-refresh>
    <cache:set-timestamp-on-error-response-storeentry>on</cache:set-timestamp-on-error-response-storeentry>
    <network:icp.llmap.rollback-peer-selection>on</network:icp.llmap.rollback-peer-selection>

    <!-- Set max-reforwards and max-reconnects to 1. -->
    <forward:availability>
      <max-reforwards>1</max-reforwards>
      <max-reconnects>1</max-reconnects>
    </forward:availability>
{code}
 

Current values caused repeated customer escalation because they impact delivery when max-reconnects values are exhausted by:
 * Netstorage origin with more than 2 replica
 * ERR_PURGE_SEQ_OUT_OF_DATE errors on properties with Netstorage origin

Past efforts to improve this values via property manager template in GSSSET-2084 and CONTROL-1025 were rejected due to lack of prioritisation and resource contraint.

GSS has to implement advance metadata to workaround these customer reports, but they are not a scalable solution. We need a product management review of these values to match customer use-cases.

summary: QR and preserve stale combination saved error response in cache
description:
||Ticket ||PM change rationale||
|ERE-2109|QR + preserve stale responses intermittently saved error response in cache. We should disallow combination setting like this. |

*Issue description :*
Customer reported that they see increase in edge 504 response from 2024-04-22 20:00 UTC. Investigation revealed that initially, 504 response is generated by edge due to ERR_PURGE_SEQ_OUT_OF_DATE. When this occurs, ghost responds to subsequent requests with 504 without making any forward requests

*Root cause:*
Due to preserve stale metadata setting ghost generated 504 on PURGE errors were saved in memory.

*Workaround:*  preserve stale metadata setting adjusted to not  save 504s

PM cache error Behavior with stale response = On + QR Combo can intermittently save ghost generated ERRORs in memory.

summary: Persisting in-region load balancing issues due to traffic spikes post SNId rollout
description:
Related Aha - 

[https://akamai.aha.io/epics/MAPPER-E-74] 

[https://akamai.aha.io/epics/COREPERF-E-26] 


These are issues that track in-region load balancing issues that continue to persist post rollout of SNId on FF/ESSL. These tickets indicate potential shortcomings with the SNId feature.

 

Top Themes - 
1) Traffic originating from cloud agents often end up using a less diverse set of NS, resulting in larger MPGs, translating to larger workload spikes. The requests from such clients are often for web/api traffic that are usually flit intensive compared to general end user traffic. Concentrated requests and high f2b traffic isn't often handled well across all regions. 

2) Synchornized traffic from certain client devices (such as STBs) result in severe overload unless the hostname is already prespread. Few customers have a time based rollout/update, that causes multiple clients across ASN/geo to make a request simultaneously. While the end users maybe spread across multiple edge regions, the sudden burst of traffic often overwhelms the initial set of machines, eventually spilling over to rest of the machines in the region. 

 

Challenges - 

1) SNId doesn't seem to address overloads when a large amount of traffic shows up. This seems to be limited by how much dnsp has a history of traffic on the serial. If the region could maintain a historic state of load on specific serials, it might be able to proactively maintain a higher min-spread for longer periods (similar to DOM), instead of deciding to quickly dial down the spread when load drops or moves elsewhere. 

2) While there have been improvements at lower percentiles, some customers expect consistent perf/stability at p99. The current measures don't seem adequate enough to guarantee this. 

3) While some cloud demand is manually broken up using topology.xml (ex: AWS) to better manage the MPG load, this reactive measure isn't a scalable approach. There needs to be a more proactive, automated way to identify newer agents and try to break them up or limit the NS grouping, to reduce the changes of larger flit traffic spikes due to large MPGs.
creator: Sandeep Jain

summary: AMD - QR issues :Default percent-to-monitor setting resulting in incomplete cached objects
description:
Issues where the default QR is not performant enough or specific QR settings might've resulted in availability or perf issues. 

Categories covered: 

1) Use of percent-to-monitor tag in the QR snippet sometimes results in incomplete object being delivered to users. This tag causes Ghost to monitor only the first 90% of the objects' bytes. When a failure/slowness does occur in the last few bytes of transfer, it goes unnoticed. QR is not triggered, resulting in partial fetch of content and eventually playback failures.
resolution_description: Media default "<percent-to-monitor>100</percent-to-monitor>" setting has been updated to 100%. While the number of object corruption issues due to this setting was low, it did cause couple of SIs in the past. With this change, we should no longer see impartial downloaded objects throughout the duration of the download as QR will now monitor for slowness for the entirety of the download and initiate an alternate request accordingly.

summary: Region overload and load misreporting due to SNId configuration issue
description:
The platform wide rollout of SNId has introduced newer themes of problems. For SNId to work, the configuration file control.snid.snitoserial.{essl|freeflow} should have the right serial/slot associated with the hostname. Currently, the hostname discovery process relies on parsing ddc logs at certain intervals and associating existing production hostnames with appropriate serial/slot. The prod config push itself is either weekly or on-demand at a lower frequency.

Below are the themes of issues noticed so far - 
1) New onboarding of large traffic hostnames requires a prior config push that includes placeholder hostnames. This could also include migration of hostnames from VIP to SNI. In such cases, the hostnames would likely have a stale association, resulting in load on an incorrect serial. 
Today, only new traffic onboarding the involves ERE consultation are proactively addressed by this method; the rest wouldn't realize until after the initial traffic has already been onboarded

2) Load reporting issues due to incorrect serial association in the SNId config -
This could happen either due to wildcard matches for certain hostnames or relying on slot number instead of the actual serials on FF. This problem also occurs when slots change or a newer iteration of the SNId config incorrectly overrides the previous entry. 

3) Manual overrides/temporary change in hostname serial (ex: prespread for events or ERE suggestion) - 
For certain traffic profiles that are deemed too spiky for SNId alone to be effective, prespread or a non-default serial is sometimes suggested. While the serials can be updated fairly quickly in portal, the current SNId config update process has a multi-day delay and results in temporary incorrect reporting of load on a serial. This also creates toil for the teams as the changes need to communicated and coordinated among multiple teams. 

SNId team is working on a more agile, automated mechanism to address some of the challenges highlighted above. Until then, this ticket tracks toil introduced due to such which should help prioritize the work.
resolution_description: The update frequency to control.snid.snitoserial.* configs has significantly increased from once a week to several times a day. There have been no noticeable customer escalations regarding mismatched hostname-serial associations since then. Closing this after monitoring this for an extended period.
creator: Sandeep Jain

summary: AMD/DD: QR inefficiencies on ICP path
description:
Tracks customer performance issues when QR was ineffective due to primary and alternate paths following the same parent hierarchy. 

*Problem statement:*

In general, the forward path for a cache-miss request looks like this: Edge -> TD0/MCH -> CW/Origin. When there's slowness on TD0/MCH parent, QR tries the alternate path: Edge -> TD0_ALT -> CW/Origin, which addresses any potential slowness seen on the primary TD0/MCH path. 

However, when the edge request is forwarded to an Edge ICP peer, the forward list for the Edge Ghost is: *Edge -> Edge_ICP -> TD0/MCH -> CW/Origin.* 

When it's an edge ICP hit and there's backend switch/peer Ghost slowness, Edge will try the next parent in the forward list, which is TD0/MCH. This still ensures we try at least two unique paths. 

However, when there's an edge ICP miss/refresh involved, ICP naturally takes the first fwd parent - TD/MCH. If this is slow, the alternate path is tried, which skips ICP, but ends up trying the next parent in the forward list, which also happens to be the same TD0/MCH path. Thus, both requests end up converging at the same forward path, while the actual TD0_ALT path is never attempted. 

 

A potential workaround involves disabling edge ICP, but that has its own implications on COGS and edge offload. If the primary request slowness is seen by an ICP peer, there needs to be a mechanism for QR to try the actual alternate path rather than the subsequent parent in the forward list.
creator: Sandeep Jain

summary: AMD/DD: QR inefficiencies due to SCM mapping
description:
Related tickets: 

[DEL-I-128|https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/DEL-I-128]

MAPPINGENG-60 / MAPPINGENG-123

[*CR 4484696*|https://bugzilla.akamai.com/show_bug.cgi?id=4484696]

 

Tracks customer performance escalations where QR was ineffective due to the choice of regions on alternate path with SCM mapping.

 

*Problem Statement:*

1) Prior to MCH as the default TD0, a fair degree of disjointedness was ensured on the primary and alternate QR/FA paths. SCM mapping (ex: ch2p and ch2f) would be aware of the primary region handed out for an edge region and would choose an alternate parent region from the same allowlist, but a distinct ecor (or region). With MCH though, the traditional SCM mapping is no longer honored. There are no primary mappoints to consider to construct an effective disjoint alternate path. The allowlist of failover map can contain regions which are also part of MCH backend. Since the primary MCH and alternate path decisions are made independently (ex: primary: mch-ff-lo, alt: ch2f) certain requests that get hashed to a particular MCH backend region could also serve as the failover path region. There's no strict 'no-overlap' check as one would expect with traditional SCM TD rendering QR/FA ineffective in such cases.

 

2) SCM failover mapping in general isn't performant enough. The failover mappoints can often be mapped to farther locations, which can probably address some extreme primary path slowness, but might not necessarily be comparable to general primary path perf.  

Sometimes, the primary and failover requests end up on the same region (even with traditional SCM pair such as ch2p/ch2f), resulting in no resiliency gain. There might be knobs ([https://collaborate.akamai.com/confluence/display/MAPENG/Failover+Mapping#FailoverMapping-GlobalRAloadbalancingoptions]) to strictly enforce this, but doesn't seem to be in place for most maprules.
creator: Sandeep Jain

summary: Availability and performance issues due to attack against customer domains
description:
[https://akamai.aha.io/ideas/ideas/MAPPER-I-40]

[https://akamai.aha.io/features/MAPPER-53]

More than 15 ERE cases. but underneath multiple customer impact including 
This tracks escalations due to platform instability (Ghost Rolls, self-suspensions) triggered by localized or large scale attack against other customer properties. While the customers' properties may or may not be protected against the attack due to their WAF policies/security features, it often tends to have unintended side effects such as machines hitting their memory limit or experiencing seg faults resulting in significant percentage of Ghosts rolling.

Depending on the nature of the attack, a swarm of requests against a customer property can hold up significant amount of resources (CPU, sockets) resulting in machines going down under load bringing the overall usable capacity down significantly. 

Currently, there aren't many options to workaround this problem other than blocking certain offending bot IPs and waiting for the attack to subside while absorbing most of the impact. 

 

On ESSL, one option is to move the customer hostname to a quarantine map. This has operational overhead and involves coordination with IAT to move properties on to a different map with limited capacity. While this can reasonably shield rest of the platform, may not be a feasible solution for larger customers and without significantly impacting them. 

Other options include limiting the spread of such traffic via internal dnsp spreadlimiter controls. 

There's active work on the CRP side which could potentially address some of the shortcomings by proactively blocking anomalous requests for certain customers. But the effectiveness is yet to be tested in prod. 

Also, There have been multiple instances where amplification of retries leads larger % of suspensions and rolls on the network when origins are down or have issues due to OHD not being default or sheer cascading timeout without adaptive mechanism.
creator: Sandeep Jain

summary: Forward Region Issues causing spike in ERR_READ_ERROR|fwdStateFree
description:
*Issue*

Multiple Customers are experiencing ERR_READ_ERROR|fwdStateFree errors and Recently there was an [SI-00023285] effecting multiple customers. Edge servers fail to forward requests due to complete forward path exhaustion, resulting in 503 errors delivered to end users without any forward attempt.

The Region health detects mid-tier regions as bad and origin IPACL has no fallback mechanism even with Acceptance criteria enabled. Actually, the Edge normally uses two tier to go forward: SIACL (primary) and SIACL-ALT (backup). SIACL tries to provide two forward paths, but both get removed because the ghost marks those regions as bad while SIACL-ALT provides one forward path, but it has strict acceptance criteria, so Ghost is not allowed to use it. With no valid forward paths left, Ghost does not trigger any forward request at all, which leads to the ERR_READ_ERROR|fwdStateFree.
